source_paper_title: Improved Bounds for Single-Nomination Impartial Selection
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cembrano_2025, title={Improved Bounds for Single-Nomination Impartial Selection}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0431}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0431}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cembrano, Javier and Fischer, Felix and Klimm, Max}, year={2025}, month=june }
context:
Page 14, Section 5 (end of the section introduction, immediately after stating Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.2).

Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach 3/4 as n goes to infinity, and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to 3/4-optimal when n is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights.
title: Achieve asymptotic three-quarters approximation for impartial selection in single-nomination graphs
background_latex:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph with \(|V|=n\), no self-loops, and exactly one outgoing edge per vertex (the single-nomination model): for every \(v\in V\) there exists a unique \(u\in V\setminus\{v\}\) with \((v,u)\in E\).

For a vertex \(v\in V\), its indegree is \(\delta^-(v,G)=|\{u\in V:(u,v)\in E\}|\). The maximum indegree is \(\Delta(G)=\max_{v\in V}\delta^-(v,G)\).

A (possibly randomized) selection mechanism is a mapping \(f\) that assigns to each such graph \(G\) a probability distribution \(f(G)\in[0,1]^V\) over vertices, with \(\sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)=1\). The expected indegree selected by \(f\) on \(G\) is
\[
\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)] = \sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)\,\delta^-(v,G).
\]

Impartiality means that a vertex cannot affect its own selection probability by changing its outgoing edge: for any two graphs \(G=(V,E)\) and \(G'=(V,E')\) on the same vertex set that differ only in the outgoing edge of a single vertex \(v\) (equivalently, \(E\setminus(\{v\}\times V)=E'\setminus(\{v\}\times V)\)), it holds that \(f_v(G)=f_v(G')\).

For \(\alpha\le 1\), mechanism \(f\) is \(\alpha\)-optimal (worst-case multiplicative guarantee) if for every graph \(G\) with \(\Delta(G)>0\),
\[
\frac{\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)]}{\Delta(G)}\ge \alpha.
\]
Equivalently, the performance guarantee of \(f\) is \(\inf_{G:\Delta(G)>0} \mathbb{E}[\delta^-(v,G)]/\Delta(G)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a sequence of impartial selection mechanisms \((f_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\), where each \(f_n\) is defined on all single-nomination directed graphs \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|V|=n\), such that the worst-case performance guarantees satisfy
\[
\liminf_{n\to\infty}\; \inf_{G:\,|V|=n,\,\Delta(G)>0}\; \frac{\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f_n(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)]}{\Delta(G)} \ge \frac{3}{4}.
\]
Equivalently: is asymptotic \(3/4\)-optimality achievable by impartial mechanisms in the single-nomination model (or can one prove a constant \(<3/4\) upper bound) as \(n\to\infty\)?
latex:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph with \(|V|=n\), no self-loops, and exactly one outgoing edge per vertex (the single-nomination model): for every \(v\in V\) there exists a unique \(u\in V\setminus\{v\}\) with \((v,u)\in E\).

Impartiality means that a vertex cannot affect its own selection probability by changing its outgoing edge: for any two graphs \(G=(V,E)\) and \(G'=(V,E')\) on the same vertex set that differ only in the outgoing edge of a single vertex \(v\) (equivalently, \(E\setminus(\{v\}\times V)=E'\setminus(\{v\}\times V)\)), it holds that \(f_v(G)=f_v(G')\).

Determine whether there exists a sequence of impartial selection mechanisms \((f_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\), where each \(f_n\) is defined on all single-nomination directed graphs \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|V|=n\), such that the worst-case performance guarantees satisfy
\[

\]

location_in_paper: Page 14, Section 5 (end of the section introduction, immediately after stating Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.2).
open_problem_quote: Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach 3/4 as n goes to infinity, and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to 3/4-optimal when n is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Disproving asymptotic 3/4-optimality would amount to finding hard families of graphs yielding an explicit universal upper bound below 3/4, which is not a simple finite search. However, for fixed n one can encode the best possible impartial mechanism as a linear program over symmetry-reduced graph types, making computational searches for improved finite-n upper bounds or extremal instances feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate mechanisms can be represented algorithmically (e.g., mixtures of impartial primitives, permutation-based rules, or LP-defined symmetric rules), and fitness can be estimated by evaluating worst-case ratios over a large adversarially generated set of graphs (or by solving an inner minimization/LP). The main difficulty is reliably approximating the true infimum over all graphs for each n, so feedback is only a proxy unless paired with exact optimization for small n.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the single-nomination model (each vertex has outdegree exactly 1), the source paper establishes the current baseline for worst-case multiplicative guarantees under impartiality. It gives a tight analysis of the permutation mechanism (Perm), proving it is exactly \(2/3\)-optimal for all \(n\), via a refined adversarial/coupling argument controlling the distribution of indegrees-from-the-left and showing a weak negative-correlation property (Lemma 3.4). It then improves the best known lower bound by constructing a new impartial mechanism (Mix) that combines Perm with a strengthened plurality-with-runner-up variant (PRUGD), achieving \(2105/3147\approx 0.6689\) for all \(n\). On the impossibility side, it derives new finite-\(n\) upper bounds for any impartial mechanism (Theorem 5.1), with worst-case minimum \(76/105\approx 0.7238\) and asymptotic upper bound tending to \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\). These results leave open whether the asymptotic optimum equals \(3/4\) or is bounded away from it.

The forward-citing works provided do not resolve the \(3/4\) question in the original prediction-free model. Two closely related papers study a prediction-augmented plurality setting and identify \(3/4\) as an upper bound on robustness even with predictions (Theorem 6.1(ii)), reinforcing \(3/4\) as a natural barrier but not ruling out \(3/4\)-optimality without predictions. Other citations are either surveys or address different variants (deterministic/weighted/inexact \((n,k)\)-selection), offering context and techniques but no improved multiplicative guarantee toward \(3/4\) in the single-nomination 1-selection setting.

Overall, with the information available here, the problem remains open: the best known lower bound is \(2105/3147\approx 0.6689\) (source paper), while the best known general upper bounds approach \(3/4\) asymptotically but do not separate \(3/4\) from below. Progress likely requires either a new impartial mechanism exploiting the functional-graph structure (cycles with in-trees) beyond permutation/runner-up ideas, or sharper impossibility constructions that convert the finite-\(n\) linear-constraint method (2-cycle with directed paths) into an asymptotic bound strictly below \(3/4\).

sources.title: Impartial Selection with Predictions
sources.theorem_or_result: For single-agent selection in plurality (single-nomination) graphs with predictions, they give an impartial randomized mechanism (the 1-permutation mechanism) that is 1-consistent and 1/2-robust (Theorem 3.3), and by mixing with the uniform permutation mechanism obtain for every \(\rho\in[0,1]\) an impartial mechanism with consistency \(\alpha=\tfrac{2}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}\rho\) and robustness \(\beta=\tfrac{2}{3}-\tfrac{1}{6}\rho\) (Corollary 3.4). They also prove an upper bound for plurality 1-selection with predictions: any impartial \(\alpha\)-consistent, \(\beta\)-robust mechanism must satisfy \(\beta\le \tfrac{3}{4}\) and \(\alpha+\beta\le \tfrac{3}{2}\) (Theorem 6.1(ii)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether, in the single-nomination model without extra input, one can achieve asymptotic \(3/4\)-optimality (or prove a constant upper bound below \(3/4\)). This paper does not improve the best known worst-case approximation for standard impartial selection on plurality graphs (it cites the known \(2/3\) and \(\approx 0.669\) guarantees) and does not construct a mechanism achieving \(3/4\) without predictions. However, it is directly related because it establishes a \(3/4\) upper bound on robustness in the *prediction-augmented* plurality setting (Theorem 6.1(ii)), showing \(3/4\) is a hard barrier even with advice, and it provides new mechanisms/trade-offs (consistency vs robustness) that interpolate between selecting the prediction and running permutation-based impartial selection. These results inform the landscape around the \(3/4\) threshold but do not resolve whether \(3/4\) is achievable in the original no-prediction model.

sources.title: Impartial Mechanisms for Selection and Ranking
sources.theorem_or_result: For plurality graphs (single-nomination, i.e., outdegree exactly 1) and selecting one vertex with access to a predicted winner, the paper proves an upper bound on robustness: any impartial randomized 1-selection mechanism with predictions that is \(\alpha\)-consistent and \(\beta\)-robust must satisfy \(\beta\le 3/4\) and \(\alpha+\beta\le 3/2\) (Theorem 6.1(ii)). It also gives mechanisms achieving, e.g., \(\alpha=1\) and \(\beta=1/2\) (Theorem 3.3), and by mixing with the uniform permutation mechanism achieves \(\alpha=2/3+(1/3)\rho\), \(\beta=2/3-(1/6)\rho\) for \(\rho\in[0,1]\) (Corollary 3.4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether asymptotic \(3/4\)-optimality is achievable for impartial selection in the single-nomination model without extra inputs. This paper studies a stronger model where the mechanism additionally receives a prediction \(\hat\imath\) of a maximum-indegree vertex, and analyzes the trade-off between consistency (when the prediction is correct) and robustness (worst-case over predictions). It does not improve the best known worst-case approximation for prediction-free mechanisms (which remains \(2/3\) for plurality via the uniform permutation mechanism), but it proves a general impossibility bound \(\beta\le 3/4\) for any impartial mechanism with predictions on plurality graphs. Thus it supports the possibility that \(3/4\) might be an upper limit even with advice, but it neither achieves \(3/4\) robustness nor rules out \(3/4\) for the original no-prediction setting.

sources.title: Deterministic impartial selection with weights
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives a deterministic impartial inexact 
(n,k)-selection mechanism for weighted votes with approximation ratio \(\alpha = 1/\lceil 2n/k\rceil\) in the well-behaved case \(b:=2n/k\in\mathbb N\) and \(b\le k/2\), and more generally (Theorem 4.1) for \(k-k\bmod 2\ge 2\sqrt n\): \[\alpha = \frac{k-k\bmod 2}{k\,\lceil 2n/(k-k\bmod 2)\rceil}.\] It also proves tightness for its mechanism (Theorem 4.4): for any \(\varepsilon>0\), Select\(_k\) is not \((1/b+\varepsilon)\)-optimal under the same parameter conditions.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns randomized impartial selection of a single vertex in the single-nomination model (outdegree exactly 1) and asks whether asymptotic \(3/4\) approximation to \(\Delta(G)\) is achievable. This paper studies a different regime: deterministic, inexact selection of up to \(k\) agents, with general weighted votes (not restricted to outdegree 1) and objective \(\sigma(f(A))/\sigma(\mathrm{Opt}_k(A))\). Its guarantees are on the order of \(k/(2n)\) (e.g., \(1/3\) when \(k\approx 2n/3\)), far below \(3/4\), and it does not analyze the single-nomination \(k=1\) objective \(\mathbb E[\delta^-(v)]/\Delta(G)\). The only direct link is contextual: it cites prior work on single-nomination impartial selection and suggests (Discussion) that restricted settings like single-nomination might allow better performance, but it provides no new bound toward \(3/4\) in that model.

sources.title: Manipulation and peer mechanisms: A survey
sources.theorem_or_result: This paper is a survey and does not prove new approximation guarantees for impartial selection in the single-nomination model. It summarizes known results, including that in the single-nomination (outdegree-1, no abstention) setting the permutation mechanism achieves approximation ratio at least \(2/3\) (citing Cembrano et al. 2023b), and that \(1/2\) is a general upper bound in broader nomination models allowing abstention.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether asymptotic \(3/4\)-optimal impartial mechanisms exist for single-nomination graphs (outdegree exactly 1). The survey discusses the same model class as part of its taxonomy and approximation-results overview, and explicitly reports the best-known bound \(2/3\) for single nominations (from the cited paper "Improved bounds for single-nomination impartial selection"). However, it does not introduce a new mechanism, improve the \(2/3\) guarantee, prove a \(<3/4\) impossibility, or otherwise resolve the \(3/4\) question.
